title: The Wakan-Wacepee, Or Sacred Dance.[81]
content:
آ آ آ آ Lo the lights in the "Teepee-Wï؟½kan!"
آ آ آ آ  'tis the night of the Wï؟½kan Wacï؟½pee.
آ آ آ آ Round and round walks the chief of the clan,
آ آ آ آ  as he rattles the sacred Ta-shï؟½-kay; [81]
آ آ آ آ Long and loud on the Chï؟½n-che-ga [81]
آ آ آ آ  beat the drummers with magical drumsticks,
آ آ آ آ And the notes of the Chï؟½-tï؟½nka [81]
آ آ آ آ  greet like the murmur of winds on the waters.
آ آ آ آ By the friction of white-cedar wood
آ آ آ آ  for the feast was a Virgin-fire [20] kindled.
آ آ آ آ They that enter the firm brotherhood
آ آ آ آ  first must fast and be cleansed by E-neï؟½-pee;[81]
آ آ آ آ And from foot-sole to crown of the head
آ آ آ آ  must they paint with the favorite colors;
آ آ آ آ For Unktï؟½hee likes bands of blood-red,
آ آ آ آ  with the stripings of blue intermingled.
آ آ آ آ In the hollow earth, dark and profound,
آ آ آ آ  Unktï؟½hee and fiery Wakï؟½nyan
آ آ آ آ Long fought, and the terrible sound
آ آ آ آ  of the battle was louder than thunder;
آ آ آ آ The mountains were heaved and around
آ آ آ آ  were scattered the hills and the boulders,
آ آ آ آ And the vast solid plains of the ground
آ آ آ آ  rose and fell like the waves of the ocean.
آ آ آ آ But the god of the waters prevailed.
آ آ آ آ  Wakï؟½n-yan escaped from the cavern,
آ آ آ آ And long on the mountains he wailed,
آ آ آ آ  and his hatred endureth forever.

آ آ آ آ When Unktï؟½hee had finished the earth,
آ آ آ آ  and the beasts and the birds and the fishes,
آ آ آ آ And men at his bidding came forth
آ آ آ آ  from the heart of the huge hollow mountains,[69]
آ آ آ آ A band chose the god from the hordes,
آ آ آ آ  and he said: "Ye are the sons of Unktï؟½hee:
آ آ آ آ Ye are lords of the beasts and the birds,
آ آ آ آ  and the fishes that swim in the waters.
آ آ آ آ But hearken ye now to my words, 
آ آ آ آ  let them sound in your bosoms forever:
آ آ آ آ Ye shall honor Unktï؟½hee and hate Wakinyan,
آ آ آ آ  the Spirit of Thunder,
آ آ آ آ For the power of Unktï؟½hee is great,
آ آ آ آ  and he laughs at the darts of Wakinyan.
آ آ آ آ Ye shall honor the Earth and the Sun, 
آ آ آ آ  for they are your father and mother; [70]
آ آ آ آ Let your prayer to the Sun be: 
آ آ آ آ  Wakï؟½n Atï؟½; on-si-md-da oheï؟½-neï؟½."[AF]
آ آ آ آ And remember the Tï؟½ku Wakï؟½n[73]
آ آ آ آ  all-pervading in earth and in ether 
آ آ آ آ Invisible ever to man,
آ آ آ آ  but He dwells in the midst of all matter;
آ آ آ آ Yea, he dwells in the heart of the stone 
آ آ آ آ  in the hard granite heart of the boulder;
آ آ آ آ Ye shall call him forever Tunkï؟½n 
آ آ آ آ  grandfather of all the Dakotas.
آ آ آ آ Ye are men that I choose for my own;
آ آ آ آ  ye shall be as a strong band of brothers,
آ آ آ آ Now I give you the magical bone
آ آ آ آ  and the magical pouch of the spirits,[AG]
آ آ آ آ And these are the laws ye shall heed:
آ آ آ آ  Ye shall honor the pouch and the giver.
آ آ آ آ Ye shall walk as twin-brothers; in need,
آ آ آ آ  one shall forfeit his life for another.
آ آ آ آ Listen not to the voice of the crow.[AH]
آ آ آ آ  Hold as sacred the wife of a brother.
آ آ آ آ Strike, and fear not the shaft of the foe,
آ آ آ آ  for the soul of the brave is immortal.
آ آ آ آ Slay the warrior in battle,
آ آ آ آ  but spare the innocent babe and the mother.
آ آ آ آ Remember a promise, beware, 
آ آ آ آ  let the word of a warrior be sacred
آ آ آ آ When a stranger arrives at the tee 
آ آ آ آ  be he friend of the band or a foeman,
آ آ آ آ Give him food; let your bounty be free;
آ آ آ آ  lay a robe for the guest by the lodge-fire;
آ آ آ آ Let him go to his kindred in peace,
آ آ آ آ  if the peace-pipe he smoke in the teepee;
آ آ آ آ And so shall your children increase,
آ آ آ آ  and your lodges shall laugh with abundance.
آ آ آ آ And long shall ye live in the land,
آ آ آ آ  and the spirits of earth and the waters
آ آ آ آ Shall come to your aid, at command,
آ آ آ آ  with the power of invisible magic.
آ آ آ آ And at last, when you journey afar 
آ آ آ آ  o'er the shining "Wanï؟½gee Ta-chï؟½n-ku,"[68]
آ آ آ آ You shall walk as a red, shining star[8]
آ آ آ آ  in the land of perpetual summer."

آ آ آ آ All the night in the teepee they sang,
آ آ آ آ  and they danced to the mighty Unktï؟½hee,
آ آ آ آ While the loud-braying Chï؟½n-che-ga rang
آ آ آ آ  and the shrill-piping flute and the rattle,
آ آ آ آ Till Anpï؟½tuwee [70] rose in the east 
آ آ آ آ  from the couch of the blushing Han-nï؟½n-na,
آ آ آ آ And thus at the dance and the feast
آ آ آ آ  sang the sons of Unktï؟½hee in chorus:

آ آ آ آ "Wa-dï؟½-ta o-hnï؟½ mi-kï؟½-ge!
آ آ آ آ Wa-dï؟½-ta o-hnï؟½ mi-kï؟½-ge!
آ آ آ آ Mini-yï؟½ta itï؟½ wakï؟½ndï؟½ makï؟½,
آ آ آ آ Atï؟½ wakï؟½n Tunkï؟½nsidï؟½n.

آ آ آ آ Tunkï؟½nsidï؟½n pejihï؟½ta wakï؟½n
آ آ آ آ Micï؟½gï؟½ he Wicï؟½gï؟½!
آ آ آ آ Miniyï؟½ta itï؟½ wakï؟½ndï؟½ makï؟½.
آ آ آ آ Taukï؟½nsidan itï؟½, nï؟½pï؟½ dï؟½-win-ta woo,
آ آ آ آ Wahutï؟½pa wan yï؟½ha, nï؟½pï؟½ dï؟½-win-ta woo."

آ آ آ آ TRANSLATION.

آ آ آ آ In red swan-down he made it for me;
آ آ آ آ In red swan-down he made it for me;
آ آ آ آ He of the water he of the mysterious face 
آ آ آ آ Gave it to me;
آ آ آ آ Sacred Father Grandfather!

آ آ آ آ Grandfather made me magical medicine.
آ آ آ آ That is true!
آ آ آ آ Being of mystery, grown in the water 
آ آ آ آ He gave it to me!
آ آ آ آ To the face of our Grandfather stretch out your hand;
آ آ آ آ Holding a quadruped, stretch out your hand!

آ آ آ آ Till high o'er the hills of the east
آ آ آ آ  Anpï؟½tuwee walked on his journey,
آ آ آ آ In secret they danced at the feast,
آ آ آ آ  and communed with the mighty Unktï؟½hee.
آ آ آ آ Then opened the door of the tee
آ آ آ آ  to the eyes of the wondering Dakotas,
آ آ آ آ And the sons of Unktï؟½hee to be,
آ آ آ آ  were endowed with the sacred Ozï؟½ha[82]
آ آ آ آ By the son of tall Wazï؟½-kutï؟½, Tamdï؟½ka,
آ آ آ آ  the chief of the Magi.
آ آ آ آ And thus since the birth-day of man 
آ آ آ آ  since he sprang from the heart of the mountains,[69]
آ آ آ آ Has the sacred "Wacï؟½pee Wakï؟½n"
آ آ آ آ  by the warlike Dakotas been honored,
آ آ آ آ And the god-favored sons of the clan
آ آ آ آ  work their will with the help of the spirits.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Hanford Lennox Gordon, titled "The Wakan-Wacepee, Or Sacred Dance.[81]", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
